I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd

# Get all Literature & Fiction books
books_query = """
SELECT book_id, title, categories 
FROM books_info 
WHERE categories LIKE '%Literature & Fiction%'
"""

# Get all reviews
reviews_query = """
SELECT purchase_id, rating
FROM review
"""

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({"books_query": books_query, "reviews_query": reviews_query}))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json

# Read the review data file
result_key = 'var_functions.query_db:16'
if isinstance(result_key, str) and result_key.endswith('.json'):
    with open(result_key, 'r') as f:
        reviews = json.load(f)
else:
    reviews = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:16', [])

# Also get the literature books
books = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:14', [])

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'reviews_count': len(reviews),
    'books_count': len(books),
    'reviews_sample': reviews[:5] if reviews else [],
    'books_sample': books[:5] if books else []
}))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import re

# Get all literature books
var_keys = list(locals().keys())
books_key = None
for key in var_keys:
    if 'books_info' in str(key).lower() or (isinstance(locals()[key], list) and len(locals()[key]) > 0 and isinstance(locals()[key][0], dict) and 'categories' in locals()[key][0]):
        books_key = key
        break

books_data = locals().get(books_key, []) if books_key else []

# Get all reviews
reviews_key = None
for key in var_keys:
    if 'review' in str(key).lower() and isinstance(locals()[key], list):
        reviews_key = key
        break

reviews_data = locals().get(reviews_key, []) if reviews_key else []

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'books_found': len(books_data),
    'reviews_found': len(reviews_data),
    'sample_book': books_data[0] if books_data else None,
    'sample_review': reviews_data[0] if reviews_data else None
}))

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import re

# Get all literature books
var_keys = list(locals().keys())
books_key = None
for key in var_keys:
    if 'books_info' in str(key).lower() or (isinstance(locals()[key], list) and len(locals()[key]) > 0 and isinstance(locals()[key][0], dict) and 'categories' in locals()[key][0]):
        books_key = key
        break

books_data = locals().get(books_key, []) if books_key else []

# Get all reviews
reviews_key = None
for key in var_keys:
    if 'review' in str(key).lower() and isinstance(locals()[key], list):
        reviews_key = key
        break

reviews_data = locals().get(reviews_key, []) if reviews_key else []

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'books_found': len(books_data),
    'reviews_found': len(reviews_data),
    'sample_book': books_data[0] if books_data else None,
    'sample_review': reviews_data[0] if reviews_data else None
}))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

import json
import pandas as pd

# Load the book data from file path
books_file = 'var_functions.query_db:36'
# Looking at the results, these appear to be file paths loaded as strings
# Let me check what's actually in the variables

# Check available variables
all_vars = list(locals().keys())
print('Available variables:', all_vars[:20])

# Try to find actual data
books_data = None
reviews_data = None

# Look for the file paths
for var_name in all_vars:
    var_value = locals()[var_name]
    if isinstance(var_value, str) and 'query_db' in var_value:
        if ':36' in var_value:
            books_data = var_value
        elif ':37' in var_value:
            reviews_data = var_value

print('Books data reference:', books_data)
print('Reviews data reference:', reviews_data)

# Read the actual files if they exist
import os

books_list = []
reviews_list = []

# Since the tool tells us these are file paths stored in variables,
# and the preview shows actual JSON content, let me try to access the actual data
# by checking if we can load from a file with the pattern

try:
    # Try a different approach - check if there are any JSON files in current dir
    files = [f for f in os.listdir('.') if f.endswith('.json')]
    print('JSON files found:', files)
    
    # Or try to reconstruct the data from what we can see
    # The query results should be accessible via the variable names
    print('Trying to access from locals...')
    for key, value in locals().items():
        if isinstance(value, list) and len(value) > 0:
            if isinstance(value[0], dict):
                if 'book_id' in value[0]:
                    books_list = value
                    print(f'Found books data in {key}, count: {len(books_list)}')
                elif 'rating' in value[0] and 'purchase_id' in value[0]:
                    reviews_list = value
                    print(f'Found reviews data in {key}, count: {len(reviews_list)}')
                    
except Exception as e:
    print('Error:', e)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'books_loaded': len(books_list),
    'reviews_loaded': len(reviews_list),
    'books_sample': books_list[0] if books_list else None
}))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
# First, let's check what variables are available
import json

all_vars = list(locals().keys())
print('All variables:', all_vars)

# Let's look for our data systematically
books_data = None
reviews_data = None

for var_name in all_vars:
    var_value = locals()[var_name]
    # Check if it's a list with dictionaries
    if isinstance(var_value, list) and len(var_value) > 0:
        if isinstance(var_value[0], dict):
            keys = var_value[0].keys()
            # Check for books data
            if 'book_id' in keys or 'categories' in keys:
                books_data = var_value
                print(f'Found books_data in variable: {var_name} count: {len(books_data)}')
            # Check for reviews data  
            elif 'rating' in keys and 'purchase_id' in keys:
                reviews_data = var_value
                print(f'Found reviews_data in variable: {var_name} count: {len(reviews_data)}')

# If no direct data found, check for file paths
if books_data is None or reviews_data is None:
    import os
    if os.path.exists('.'):
        files = os.listdir('.')
        json_files = [f for f in files if f.endswith('.json')]
        print('JSON files:', json_files)

print('Final counts:')
print('Books:', len(books_data) if books_data else 0)
print('Reviews:', len(reviews_data) if reviews_data else 0)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'books_found': len(books_data) if books_data else 0,
    'reviews_found': len(reviews_data) if reviews_data else 0
}))

args.code:
import json

# Get all variables
all_vars = locals()

# Find books data
books_data = None
for key, value in all_vars.items():
    if isinstance(value, list) and len(value) > 0:
        if isinstance(value[0], dict) and 'book_id' in value[0]:
            books_data = value
            print(f'Found books data: {len(books_data)} books')
            break

# Find reviews data
reviews_data = None  
for key, value in all_vars.items():
    if isinstance(value, list) and len(value) > 0:
        if isinstance(value[0], dict) and 'rating' in value[0] and 'purchase_id' in value[0]:
            reviews_data = value
            print(f'Found reviews data: {len(reviews_data)} reviews')
            break

if books_data and reviews_data:
    # Extract purchase IDs from reviews
    purchase_ids = set()
    for review in reviews_data:
        purchase_ids.add(review['purchase_id'])
    
    print(f'Unique purchase IDs in reviews: {len(purchase_ids)}')
    print('Sample purchase IDs:', list(purchase_ids)[:10])
    
    # Extract book IDs from books
    book_ids = set()
    for book in books_data:
        book_ids.add(book['book_id'])
    
    print(f'Book IDs in literature: {len(book_ids)}')
    print('Sample book IDs:', list(book_ids)[:10])

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({'books': len(books_data) if books_data else 0, 'reviews': len(reviews_data) if reviews_data else 0}))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json

# Get all variables
all_vars = locals()

# Find books data
books_data = None
for key, value in all_vars.items():
    if isinstance(value, list) and len(value) > 0:
        if isinstance(value[0], dict) and 'book_id' in value[0]:
            books_data = value
            print(f'Found books data: {len(books_data)} books')
            break

# Find reviews data
reviews_data = None  
for key, value in all_vars.items():
    if isinstance(value, list) and len(value) > 0:
        if isinstance(value[0], dict) and 'rating' in value[0] and 'purchase_id' in value[0]:
            reviews_data = value
            print(f'Found reviews data: {len(reviews_data)} reviews')
            break

if books_data and reviews_data:
    # Extract purchase IDs from reviews
    purchase_ids = set()
    for review in reviews_data:
        purchase_ids.add(review['purchase_id'])
    
    print(f'Unique purchase IDs in reviews: {len(purchase_ids)}')
    print('Sample purchase IDs:', list(purchase_ids)[:10])
    
    # Extract book IDs from books
    book_ids = set()
    for book in books_data:
        book_ids.add(book['book_id'])
    
    print(f'Book IDs in literature: {len(book_ids)}')
    print('Sample book IDs:', list(book_ids)[:10])

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({'books': len(books_data) if books_data else 0, 'reviews': len(reviews_data) if reviews_data else 0}))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

args.code:
import json
import os

# The variables contain file paths
books_data = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:50')
reviews_data = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:51')

# Debug: print the type and first few chars
print('Books data type:', type(books_data))
print('Reviews data type:', type(reviews_data))
print('Books data preview:', str(books_data)[:200] if books_data else None)
print('Reviews data preview:', str(reviews_data)[:200] if reviews_data else None)

# Find actual files
files = os.listdir('.')
json_files = [f for f in files if f.endswith('.json')]
print('JSON files:', json_files)

# Load data from files
books_list = []
reviews_list = []

for file in json_files:
    try:
        with open(file, 'r') as f:
            data = json.load(f)
            if isinstance(data, list) and len(data) > 0:
                if isinstance(data[0], dict):
                    if 'book_id' in data[0]:
                        books_list = data
                        print(f'Loaded {len(books_list)} books from {file}')
                    elif 'rating' in data[0] and 'purchase_id' in data[0]:
                        reviews_list = data
                        print(f'Loaded {len(reviews_list)} reviews from {file}')
    except Exception as e:
        print(f'Error loading {file}: {e}')

print('Final counts:')
print('Books:', len(books_list))
print('Reviews:', len(reviews_list))

# Show sample data
if books_list:
    print('Sample book:', books_list[0])
if reviews_list:
    print('Sample review:', reviews_list[0])

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'books': len(books_list), 
    'reviews': len(reviews_list),
    'sample_book': books_list[0] if books_list else None
}))

print('Books data type:', type(books_data))
print('Reviews data type:', type(reviews_data))
print('Books data preview:', str(books_data)[:200] if books_data else None)
print('Reviews data preview:', str(reviews_data)[:200] if reviews_data else None)

# Find actual files
files = os.listdir('.')
json_files = [f for f in files if f.endswith('.json')]
print('JSON files:', json_files)

# Load data from files
books_list = []
reviews_list = []

for file in json_files:
    try:
        with open(file, 'r') as f:
            data = json.load(f)
            if isinstance(data, list) and len(data) > 0:
                if isinstance(data[0], dict):
                    if 'book_id' in data[0]:
                        books_list = data
                        print(f'Loaded {len(books_list)} books from {file}')
                    elif 'rating' in data[0] and 'purchase_id' in data[0]:
                        reviews_list = data
                        print(f'Loaded {len(reviews_list)} reviews from {file}')
    except Exception as e:
        print(f'Error loading {file}: {e}')

print('Final counts:')
print('Books:', len(books_list))
print('Reviews:', len(reviews_list))

# Show sample data
if books_list:
    print('Sample book:', books_list[0])
if reviews_list:
    print('Sample review:', reviews_list[0])

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'books': len(books_list), 
    'reviews': len(reviews_list),
    'sample_book': books_list[0] if books_list else None
}))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
from collections import defaultdict

# Get books data
books_result = None
reviews_result = None

# Check variables directly
for key, value in list(locals().items()):
    if isinstance(value, list):
        if len(value) > 0 and isinstance(value[0], dict):
            if 'book_id' in value[0]:
                books_result = value
                print(f'Found books in {key}: {len(books_result)} books')
            elif 'rating' in value[0] and 'purchase_id' in value[0]:
                reviews_result = value
                print(f'Found reviews in {key}: {len(reviews_result)} reviews')

if books_result and reviews_result:
    # Create DataFrames
    df_books = pd.DataFrame(books_result)
    df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews_result)
    
    # Convert rating to float
    df_reviews['rating'] = df_reviews['rating'].astype(float)
    
    # Group reviews by purchase_id to calculate average rating
    review_stats = df_reviews.groupby('purchase_id').agg({
        'rating': ['mean', 'count']
    }).round(2)
    
    review_stats.columns = ['avg_rating', 'review_count']
    review_stats = review_stats.reset_index()
    
    # Find books with perfect 5.0 average rating
    perfect_ratings = review_stats[review_stats['avg_rating'] == 5.0]
    
    print(f'Books with perfect 5.0 rating: {len(perfect_ratings)}')
    print('Sample perfect rating books:', perfect_ratings.head())
    
    # The task is to find which Literature & Fiction books have perfect 5.0
    # We need to link purchase_id to book_id
    # From the hint, purchase_id and book_id refer to same entities but might need fuzzy matching
    
    # Extract numeric IDs
    perfect_ids = []
    for pid in perfect_ratings['purchase_id']:
        # Extract number from purchaseid_X
        try:
            num = int(pid.replace('purchaseid_', ''))
            perfect_ids.append(num)
        except:
            pass
    
    print('Perfect rating numeric IDs:', perfect_ids[:10])
    
    # Find matching book IDs
    literature_book_ids = []
    for book in books_result:
        try:
            book_num = int(book['book_id'].replace('bookid_', ''))
            literature_book_ids.append(book_num)
        except:
            pass
    
    print('Literature book numeric IDs (sample):', literature_book_ids[:10])
    
    # Find intersection
    perfect_literature_ids = set(perfect_ids) & set(literature_book_ids)
    print('Perfect literature book count:', len(perfect_literature_ids))
    
    # Get actual book data for these
    perfect_books = []
    for book in books_result:
        try:
            book_num = int(book['book_id'].replace('bookid_', ''))
            if book_num in perfect_literature_ids:
                # Find review stats for this book
                purchase_id = f'purchaseid_{book_num}'
                stats = review_stats[review_stats['purchase_id'] == purchase_id]
                if not stats.empty:
                    book_info = {
                        'book_id': book['book_id'],
                        'title': book.get('title', ''),
                        'categories': book.get('categories', ''),
                        'avg_rating': float(stats.iloc[0]['avg_rating']),
                        'review_count': int(stats.iloc[0]['review_count'])
                    }
                    perfect_books.append(book_info)
        except:
            pass
    
    print('Final matching books:', len(perfect_books))
    
    print('__RESULT__:')
    print(json.dumps(perfect_books))
else:
    print('__RESULT__:')
    print(json.dumps({'error': 'Data not found', 'books': len(books_result) if books_result else 0, 'reviews': len(reviews_result) if reviews_result else 0}))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd

# Get all books data
all_vars = locals()
books_data = None
reviews_data = None

for key, value in all_vars.items():
    if isinstance(value, list) and len(value) > 0:
        if isinstance(value[0], dict):
            if 'book_id' in value[0]:
                books_data = value
                print(f'Found books data: {len(books_data)} books')
            elif 'rating' in value[0] and 'purchase_id' in value[0]:
                reviews_data = value
                print(f'Found reviews data: {len(reviews_data)} reviews')

# Get the specific books we identified as having perfect 5.0 rating
target_book_ids = ['bookid_13', 'bookid_36', 'bookid_38', 'bookid_106', 'bookid_177', 'bookid_187']

# Filter books
matching_books = []
for book in books_data:
    if book['book_id'] in target_book_ids:
        matching_books.append(book)

print(f'Found {len(matching_books)} matching books')

# Create better structured output
results = []
for book in matching_books:
    # Parse the categories string to check it's Literature & Fiction
    categories_str = book.get('categories', '')
    is_lit_fiction = 'Literature & Fiction' in categories_str
    
    # Get book title
    title = book.get('title', '')
    
    # Find review stats
    book_num = book['book_id'].replace('bookid_', '')
    purchase_id = f'purchaseid_{book_num}'
    
    # Calculate rating stats
    book_reviews = [r for r in reviews_data if r.get('purchase_id') == purchase_id]
    if book_reviews:
        ratings = [float(r['rating']) for r in book_reviews]
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        review_count = len(ratings)
        
        if avg_rating == 5.0 and is_lit_fiction:
            results.append({
                'title': title,
                'book_id': book['book_id'],
                'average_rating': round(avg_rating, 1),
                'review_count': review_count,
                'categories': categories_str
            })

print(f'Final results: {len(results)} books')
for r in results:
    print(f"- {r['title']} (Rating: {r['average_rating']}, Reviews: {r['review_count']})")

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(results, indent=2))

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:5.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:5.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:5.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!
